amortized: Average cost per operation over a sequence of operations, even if some individual operations are expensive. E.g. dynamic array resizing is O(1) amortized.
tail latency: Latency at the high end of a distribution (e.g. p99, p999). Even rare slow responses matter at scale.
idempotent: An operation that produces the same result regardless of how many times it's applied. Safe to retry.
memoization: Caching the result of a function call so the same inputs return the cached result instead of recomputing.
hashing: Mapping data of arbitrary size to fixed-size values using a hash function. Used in hash tables, checksums, bloom filters.
collision: When two distinct keys map to the same hash bucket. Handled via chaining or open addressing.
sharding: Partitioning data across multiple nodes so each node holds a subset. Enables horizontal scaling.
replication: Keeping copies of data on multiple nodes for fault tolerance and read throughput.
consensus: Agreement among distributed nodes on a single value, despite failures. E.g. Raft, Paxos.
eventual consistency: A consistency model where replicas converge to the same value given enough time and no new updates.
strong consistency: Every read reflects the most recent write. Requires coordination across replicas.
linearizability: The strongest consistency guarantee: operations appear instantaneous and in real-time order.
backpressure: A mechanism where a consumer signals a producer to slow down to avoid being overwhelmed.
throughput: The amount of work completed per unit of time. E.g. requests per second.
latency: Time taken to complete a single operation from start to finish.
big-o: Asymptotic notation describing the upper bound on an algorithm's time or space complexity as input grows.
recursion: A function that calls itself with a smaller subproblem until reaching a base case.
dynamic programming: Breaking a problem into overlapping subproblems, solving each once, and storing results to avoid recomputation.
greedy: An algorithm strategy that makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping it leads to a global optimum.
divide and conquer: Recursively splitting a problem into independent subproblems, solving them, then combining results.
invariant: A condition that remains true throughout the execution of a program or loop.
predicate: A boolean-valued function or condition used to test a property.
heap: A tree-based data structure satisfying the heap property: parent ≥ children (max-heap) or parent ≤ children (min-heap).
trie: A tree where each node represents a character prefix. Enables O(m) string lookups where m is key length.
bloom filter: A probabilistic data structure that tests set membership. May produce false positives, never false negatives.
consistent hashing: A hashing scheme where adding/removing nodes minimizes key remapping. Used in distributed caches.
load balancing: Distributing incoming requests across multiple servers to avoid hotspots.
cache eviction: The process of removing entries from a cache when capacity is reached. Common policies: LRU, LFU, FIFO.
lru: Least Recently Used - evicts the item that was accessed furthest in the past.
two-pointer: A technique using two indices that move through a data structure, often from opposite ends, to solve problems in O(n).
